description: Candidate queue for future Omega distillation sources. Oracle may enrich discovery seeds, but Codex+Claude still gate writeback.

candidates.proposed_source: Stallings foldings and Bestvina-Handel train tracks
candidates.rationale: This is the most literal next source for the folding seed: it supplies named fold moves, termination measures, canonical core objects, and group reconstruction in a compact proof corpus.
source_material: John R. Stallings, 'Topology of Finite Graphs' (Inventiones Mathematicae, 1983)
Mladen Bestvina and Michael Handel, 'Train Tracks and Automorphisms of Free Groups' (Annals of Mathematics, 1992)
Ilya Kapovich and Alexei Myasnikov, 'Stallings Foldings and Subgroups of Free Groups' (Journal of Algebra, 2002)
Karen Vogtmann, survey material on Outer space and automorphisms of free groups
risks: Some overlap with the completed Gromov source through geometric group theory themes.
The strongest theorems are free-group-specific, so Stage R should extract the fold invariant rather than overgeneralize the group setting.
candidates.first_distillation_prompt: Run Stage R on Stallings graph foldings and Bestvina-Handel train-track maps: extract a theorem schema in which local illegal-turn identifications monotonically reduce a complexity measure while preserving the represented subgroup or automorphism, then write back the residual-time invariant that certifies termination and canonical normal form.
candidates.outreach_angle: Folding becomes a visible algorithm: pictures of labeled graphs collapsing into canonical cores can support both proof writebacks and a public-facing explanation of how local identifications create global algebraic structure.

candidates.proposed_source: Hrushovski-Zilber Zariski geometries and Elekes-Szabo group extraction
candidates.rationale: It directly answers both emergent-arithmetic seeds and the Wang-Zahl group-unification seed by supplying a mature bridge from incidence geometry to definable algebraic structure.
source_material: Ehud Hrushovski and Boris Zilber, 'Zariski Geometries' (Bulletin of the American Mathematical Society, 1993)
Boris Zilber, 'Zariski Geometries: Geometry from the Logician's Point of View' (London Mathematical Society Lecture Note Series 360, 2010)
Ehud Hrushovski, 'Stable Group Theory and Approximate Subgroups' (2009)
Gyorgy Elekes and Endre Szabo, 'How to Find Groups? (and How to Use Them in Erdos Geometry?)' (Combinatorica, 2012)
risks: Model-theoretic prerequisites may make Stage R proof extraction slower.
If treated only as incidence combinatorics, it could partially duplicate Wang-Zahl; keep the focus on definable group and field reconstruction.
candidates.first_distillation_prompt: Run Stage R on Zariski geometries, the group-configuration theorem, and Elekes-Szabo incidence results: distill a theorem schema where repeated incidence constraints force a definable group or field, then write back the emergent-arithmetic mechanism as arithmetic reconstructed from geometry rather than assumed at the start.
candidates.outreach_angle: This source can frame a new-paper direction around the slogan that enough incidence is secretly algebra, connecting combinatorics, logic, and arithmetic in a way that is legible beyond model theory.

candidates.rationale: The queue has two emergent-arithmetic seeds after Grothendieck and Wang-Zahl; this source is a compact, concrete bridge between topos geometry, tropical algebra, and arithmetic dynamics.
source_material: Alain Connes and Caterina Consani, 'The Arithmetic Site' (Comptes Rendus Mathematique, 2014)
Alain Connes and Caterina Consani, 'Geometry of the Arithmetic Site' (Advances in Mathematics, 2016)
Alain Connes and Caterina Consani, papers on characteristic-one algebra and the scaling site
Background: idempotent semirings, tropical integers, Frobenius correspondences, and adele class space
risks: High conceptual overlap with Grothendieck-style topos language, although the source is a distinct characteristic-one and noncommutative geometry program.
Some claims around the Riemann zeta motivation are outreach-sensitive and should be stated as programmatic unless Stage R extracts precise theorems.
candidates.first_distillation_prompt: Run Stage R on the Connes-Consani arithmetic site: extract how a topos over the multiplicative monoid of positive integers, with tropical characteristic-one structure sheaves and Frobenius correspondences, realizes arithmetic as geometry; write back a theorem template for emergent arithmetic from site-theoretic dynamics.
candidates.outreach_angle: The arithmetic site gives a striking narrative for new papers: numbers become a space, Frobenius becomes motion, and zeta behavior becomes a geometric counting problem.

candidates.proposed_source: Persistent homology, persistence modules, and constructible sheaf persistence
candidates.rationale: Three independent POM seeds point toward cover-scale memory after Bourgain, Cech cohomology, and Gromov; persistence is the cleanest existing theorem technology for that gap.
source_material: Herbert Edelsbrunner, David Letscher, and Afra Zomorodian, 'Topological Persistence and Simplification' (Discrete and Computational Geometry, 2002)
Afra Zomorodian and Gunnar Carlsson, 'Computing Persistent Homology' (Discrete and Computational Geometry, 2005)
William Crawley-Boevey, 'Decomposition of Pointwise Finite-Dimensional Persistence Modules' (Journal of Algebra and Its Applications, 2015)
Masaki Kashiwara and Pierre Schapira, 'Sheaves on Manifolds' (1990), plus Justin Curry's sheaf and cosheaf dissertation for applied persistence
risks: POM is not expanded in the queue snapshot, so Stage R should first pin the intended interpretation before broad extraction.
Standard barcode results are well known; novelty should come from translating persistence into Omega writebacks, not restating TDA folklore.
candidates.first_distillation_prompt: Run Stage R on persistent homology and constructible sheaf persistence: model POM as a module of features or obstructions over a filtration, prove the interval-decomposition or barcode theorem in the one-parameter case, then write back the Omega rule that persistent classes are the stable residues of changing covers.
candidates.outreach_angle: Persistence gives diagrams, barcodes, and stable invariants, making POM explainable as a measurable memory of structure across scales rather than as an internal abstraction.

candidates.proposed_source: Connes spectral triples and Chamseddine-Connes spectral action
candidates.rationale: The physical-spacetime-skeleton seed needs a concrete theorem source; spectral triples give one of the clearest mathematical pipelines from algebraic data to metric and field skeletons.
source_material: Alain Connes, 'Noncommutative Geometry' (1994)
Alain Connes, 'Noncommutative Geometry and Reality' (1995)
Ali H. Chamseddine and Alain Connes, 'The Spectral Action Principle' (Communications in Mathematical Physics, 1997)
Alain Connes and Matilde Marcolli, 'Noncommutative Geometry, Quantum Fields and Motives' (2008)
risks: The physics-facing material can invite overclaiming; keep Stage R grounded in reconstruction theorems, spectral triples, and precise action formulas.
There is conceptual adjacency to the arithmetic-site candidate, so Stage R should separate spacetime reconstruction from characteristic-one arithmetic.
candidates.first_distillation_prompt: Run Stage R on Connes spectral triples and the Chamseddine-Connes spectral action: extract a theorem schema where a space is reconstructed from an algebra, a Hilbert space, and a Dirac operator, then write back the physical-spacetime-skeleton mechanism as metric, dimension, and field content encoded by spectra.
candidates.outreach_angle: Spectral triples supply a strong outreach story: hear the shape of spacetime, then recover geometry and gauge structure from operator data.

candidates.proposed_source: Ecalle resurgence, mould calculus, and alien derivatives
candidates.rationale: The fold_residual_time seeds need a source where residual layers are first-class mathematical objects; resurgence is unusually rich in explicit residual operators and temporal transition data.
source_material: Jean Ecalle, 'Les Fonctions Resurgentes', volumes I to III (Publications Mathematiques d'Orsay, 1981 to 1985)
Jean Ecalle, 'Introduction aux Fonctions Analysables et Preuve Constructive de la Conjecture de Dulac' (1992)
David Sauzin, expository notes on resurgence theory and mould calculus
Core concepts: Borel-Laplace summation, Stokes automorphisms, alien derivatives, bridge equations
risks: The original sources are technically dense and notation-heavy.
The fit to fold_residual_time is conceptual rather than literal, so Stage R should target one accessible theorem chain, such as Stokes automorphisms and bridge equations.
candidates.first_distillation_prompt: Run Stage R on Ecalle resurgence and alien calculus: extract a theorem template in which divergent formal expansions are not errors but compressed temporal data whose alien derivatives expose hidden Stokes jumps; write back fold_residual_time as the structured residue left by asymptotic folding.
candidates.outreach_angle: Resurgence offers a compelling new-paper direction around mathematical memory: apparently divergent traces carry recoverable future corrections and hidden transition data.

candidates.proposed_source: Breuillard-Green-Tao approximate groups and Helfgott growth
candidates.rationale: The Wang-Zahl group-unification seed calls for a post-incidence algebra source; approximate groups provide a mature theorem package where partial closure becomes global structure.
source_material: H. A. Helfgott, 'Growth and Generation in SL_2(Z/pZ)' (Annals of Mathematics, 2008)
Emmanuel Breuillard, Ben Green, and Terence Tao, 'The Structure of Approximate Groups' (Publications Mathematiques de l'IHES, 2012)
László Pyber and Endre Szabó, 'Growth in Finite Simple Groups of Lie Type of Bounded Rank' (Journal of the American Mathematical Society, 2016)
Background: Freiman-Ruzsa theory, product theorems, nilprogressions, and Lie-model correspondence principles
risks: Some background overlaps with Gromov-style virtual nilpotence and Bourgain-adjacent expansion themes.
The emergent-arithmetic fit is secondary unless Stage R emphasizes small-doubling arithmetic models and product-growth dichotomies.
candidates.first_distillation_prompt: Run Stage R on approximate groups and growth in linear groups: extract a theorem schema where small doubling forces approximate algebraic closure and nilpotent or Lie-model structure, then write back group_unification as the passage from combinatorial growth constraints to unified group geometry.
candidates.outreach_angle: Approximate groups connect finite combinatorics with Lie structure and expansion; they can support a paper direction about how weak closure laws harden into algebraic symmetry.

candidates.proposed_source: Khovanskii, Newton polyhedra, fewnomials, and tropical skeletons
candidates.rationale: It gives a concrete bridge from Wang-Zahl-style polynomial geometry to arithmetic skeletons: supports, exponents, valuations, and lattice polytopes become the main objects.
source_material: A. G. Khovanskii, 'Newton Polyhedra and Toroidal Varieties' (Functional Analysis and Its Applications, 1977)
A. G. Khovanskii, 'Fewnomials' (AMS Translations of Mathematical Monographs 88, 1991)
I. M. Gelfand, M. M. Kapranov, and A. V. Zelevinsky, 'Discriminants, Resultants, and Multidimensional Determinants' (1994)
Grigory Mikhalkin, 'Enumerative Tropical Algebraic Geometry in R^2' (Journal of the American Mathematical Society, 2005)
risks: The cluster is broad; Stage R should begin with Newton polyhedra and fewnomial bounds before moving to modern tropical enumerative geometry.
Physical-spacetime-skeleton relevance is indirect and should be treated as secondary to emergent_arithmetic.
candidates.first_distillation_prompt: Run Stage R on Khovanskii fewnomials, Newton polyhedra, and tropicalization: extract a theorem schema where algebraic equations collapse to integer polyhedral skeletons through valuations, then write back emergent_arithmetic as the arithmetic lattice left visible after analytic or algebraic data are folded to their Newton complexes.
candidates.outreach_angle: Newton polytopes and tropical shadows give visual, computable skeletons; they are useful for explaining how arithmetic grids can emerge from continuous algebraic geometry.
